{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}. {{Commonscat}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Nagano Prefecture}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sarashina Shrine}} [[Category:Sarashina Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sarashina Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sarashina-jinja 1.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, Wakamiya 2 | coordinates = {{Coord|36|29|14.7|N|138|8|31.08|E|scale:30000}} | deities = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} | founded = | main_hall= | other_name = Wakamiya Hachiman | main_festival = September 23 | events = }} '''Sarashina Shrine''' (さらしなじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chikuma|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, and under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it held the status of a ''gōsha'' (village shrine). == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊}} * {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|気長足姫尊}} * {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|大鷦鷯命}} == History == According to shrine tradition, it was initially enshrined by {{ill|Prince Sakaibe no Kurohiko|ja|坂合黒彦皇子}} on a ridge of the Sarashina mountains. However, following a landslide caused by an earthquake in the third year of the {{ill|Ninna era|ja|仁和}} (887 CE), the shrine was relocated to the mountain's base. The shrine is referred to as "Wakamiya" in the {{ill|Heike Monogatari|ja|平家物語}}, specifically in relation to an event in the first year of the {{ill|Yōwa era|ja|養和}} when {{ill|Kiso Yoshinaka|ja|木曽義仲}} reportedly "prostrated himself before Hachiman Shrine." It was known as Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine until the {{ill|Hōreki era|ja|宝暦}} (1757 CE), when it assumed its current name. By 1759, records of the {{ill|Matsushiro Domain|ja|松代藩}} noted the shrine’s landholdings at three ''koku''. Before the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism), the shrine was associated with a {{ill|Shingon sect temple|ja|真言宗}} known as Wakamiya Hachiman Jingū-ji. File:Sarashina-jinja torii.jpg|Torii File:Sarashina-jinja 2.jpg|Main shrine building File:Sarashina-jinja cenotaph.jpg|{{ill|Cenotaph for loyal souls|ja|忠魂碑}} * '''Treasure Tower (Hōkyōintō):''' The grounds contain an ancient stone pagoda inscribed with the year {{ill|Eiwa era|ja|永和}} 2 (1378 CE). It is believed to mark the burial site of 45 local warriors who pledged their loyalty to Prince {{ill|Morinaga|ja|宗良親王}} and prepared for death before heading to battle. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Earthworks:''' The northern and eastern sides of the shrine grounds retain traces of medieval fortifications, possibly linked to a {{ill|Southern Court (Japan)|ja|南朝}} loyalist stronghold. * '''Slender Bronze Sword:''' Discovered during the Edo period in the nearby {{ill|Yazuka Ruins|ja|箭塚遺跡}}, this sword became a sacred treasure of the shrine. It drew attention from historians for demonstrating that bronze cultures from {{ill|Northern Kyushu|ja|北九州}} extended far beyond the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|近畿圏}}. It was designated a tangible cultural property of the city in 1988. * '''Torii Gate:''' The current torii faces east towards the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}}, which historically marked the rear of the shrine. The traditional approach was from the west, via the mountains and the ancient Hachioji-san Manji Pass. The shrine once served the worship needs of the three former villages of Wakamiya, Kurohiko, and Shibahara. Of these, Kurohiko Village, with a population of 1,000 non-agricultural households, was reportedly devastated and dispersed by frequent floods during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}. The names of Kurohiko and Wakamiya are thought to derive from the deeds of the two princes linked to the shrine's origins. == References == == Bibliography == * ''Shrines and Temples in the Shinano Cultural Series,'' 1981, {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞社}}. * ''{{ill|Kadokawa Japan Place names Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典}} Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture,'' 1990, {{ill|Kadokawa
